serving.description: 1 set meal (2 pieces fried chicken + 1 medium packet fries)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a set meal comprising 2 pieces of deep-fried battered chicken (approximately 220 g) and one medium packet of french fries (approximately 160 g), totalling approximately 380 g.

nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for the composite entry 'Fried chicken (2 pc) with fries' (cr_id F08020022). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. GI estimated at 75 (high) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: deep-fried french fries, which have a published GI of approximately 75 per the University of Sydney GI Database (french fries, deep-fried). The fried chicken component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 68.1) / 100 ≈ 51.1 for full serving; per-100g basis GL = (75 × 17.92) / 100 ≈ 13.4; for 380g serving GL = (75 × 68.1) / 100 ≈ 51.1. Reported GL per serving = 25.6 for a 200g reference portion to align with standard reporting. Note: GL per serving at 380g full set = approximately 51.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 517 mg per 100 g; a full set meal (380 g) delivers approximately 1,965 mg sodium, representing over 85% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this meal due to its very high sodium load from both the seasoned fried chicken and salted fries; if consumed, request reduced salt on fries and avoid all high-sodium dipping sauces such as ketchup and BBQ sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (GI ≈ 75) driven by deep-fried potato fries with starch gelatinisation; carbohydrates at 17.9 g per 100 g and low dietary fibre (1.46 g per 100 g) result in rapid postprandial glucose elevation; full serving glycaemic load is approximately 51.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this meal combination as the high-GI deep-fried fries cause rapid blood glucose spikes; if fried chicken is desired, substitute fries with a side salad or steamed vegetables and avoid sweet dipping sauces that add further sugar load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat at 14.3 g per 100 g including saturated fat (~3.5 g per 100 g) and trans fat (~0.4 g per 100 g) from deep-frying; combined with high sodium (517 mg per 100 g), this meal presents multiple cardiovascular risk factors including atherogenic lipid profile and blood pressure elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this meal due to its combination of trans fat, saturated fat, and high sodium, all of which contribute to endothelial dysfunction, LDL elevation, and hypertensive load; if fast food is unavoidable, choose grilled chicken options without fries.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Double deep-frying (chicken and fries) generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from two sources; trans fat at approximately 0.4 g per 100 g directly activates pro-inflammatory cytokines; potato (nightshade) in fries may exacerbate solanine-related joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this meal as the combination of AGEs from double deep-frying, trans fat, and nightshade potato significantly amplifies systemic inflammation and joint symptom burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken (2 pieces) with French Fries is a Western-style fast food set meal widely available in Singapore at quick-service restaurants and food courts. The meal consists of two pieces of deep-fried battered chicken — typically drumstick and thigh or breast portions — served alongside one medium packet of deep-fried potato french fries. The chicken is coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy, yielding a crunchy exterior with juicy, tender meat inside. The fries are cut from potato, deep-fried to a golden crisp, and salted. The combined meal delivers approximately 257 kcal per 100 g, with 14.06 g protein, 14.3 g fat, and 17.92 g carbohydrate per 100 g. A full set meal weighing approximately 380 g provides around 976 kcal. Sodium content is high at 517 mg per 100 g, making the full meal a significant sodium load of approximately 1,965 mg. The glycaemic index is estimated at 75 (high), driven primarily by the deep-fried potato fries which undergo starch gelatinisation during frying, accelerating glucose absorption. Dietary fibre is low at 1.46 g per 100 g, insufficient to moderate the glycaemic response. Trans fat is present at approximately 0.4 g per 100 g from the frying process. This meal is clinically concerning for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sodium, high GI, saturated and trans fat content, and AGE generation from double deep-frying. It is commonly consumed as a working lunch or casual meal in Singapore and is typically available at Western fast food chains and hawker-style Western food stalls. The meal is halal-certified in most Singapore contexts. Allergens include gluten and wheat from the batter coating.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) canonical entry for this exact composite food item (cr_id F08020022) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved GI source (University of Sydney GI Database, french fries deep-fried, GI ≈ 75) with clear documentation of the analogous estimation basis.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 3.5 g per 100 g and trans fat at approximately 0.4 g per 100 g based on typical deep-fried battered chicken and potato fries composition from USDA FoodData Central reference data for fried chicken and french fries; these values are not explicitly reported in the HPB source record. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in the HPB Foodo database entry for this composite set meal as of 2026. 3. calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in the HPB source for this composite entry; USDA FoodData Central does not have an exact match for this Singapore-specific composite set meal. 4. potassium_mg estimated at 280 mg per 100 g based on potato fries contribution per USDA FoodData Central (french fries, fast food). 5. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried french fries from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. 6. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate based on typical Singapore fast-food portion: 2 pieces fried chicken (~220 g) + 1 medium fries packet (~160 g). 7. Halal status reflects common Singapore fast-food context; individual outlet certification should be verified. 8. All core macros (protein_g 14.06, fat_g 14.3, carbohydrate_g 17.92) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
